---
title: "ISA 401: Business Intelligence & Data Visualization"
subtitle: '09: Tidy Data &amp; Validation in `r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("r-project"), fill = "white")`'
author: '<br>Fadel M. Megahed, PhD <br><br>Raymond E. Gloss Professor in Business <br> Farmer School of Business<br> Miami University<br><br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("linkedin"), fill = "white")` @Fadel Megahed](https://www.linkedin.com/in/fadel-megahed-289046b4) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("github"), fill = "white")` fmegahed](https://github.com/fmegahed/) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("paper-plane", style = "solid"), fill = "white")` fmegahed@miamioh.edu](mailto:fmegahed@miamioh.edu)<br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("question"), fill = "white")` Automated Scheduler for Office Hours](https://calendly.com/fmegahed)<br><br>'
date: "Spring 2026"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    self_contained: true
    css: [default, "../../style_files/fonts.css", "../../style_files/my-theme.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: magula
      highlightLines: true
      highlightLanguage: ["r"]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: "16:9"
header-includes:
  - "../../style_files/header.html"
---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = FALSE,
                      echo = TRUE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      progress = FALSE,
                      verbose = FALSE,
                      dev = 'png',
                      fig.height = 2.5,
                      dpi = 300,
                      fig.align = 'center')

options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)

miamired = '#C3142D'

if(require(pacman)==FALSE) install.packages("pacman")
if(require(devtools)==FALSE) install.packages("devtools")

if(require(countdown)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/countdown")
if(require(xaringanExtra)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/xaringanExtra")


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, magrittr, lubridate, janitor, # data analysis pkgs
               tidyr, pointblank, # for tidy data and validation
               fontawesome, RefManageR, xaringanExtra, countdown) # for slides
```

```{r xaringan-themer, include=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
if(require(xaringanthemer) == FALSE) install.packages("xaringanthemer")
library(xaringanthemer)

xaringanthemer::style_mono_accent(base_color = "#84d6d3",
                  base_font_size = "20px")

xaringanExtra::use_xaringan_extra(c("tile_view", "animate_css", "tachyons", "panelset", "search", "fit_screen", "editable",
                                    "clipable"))
```


## Quick Refresher from Last Week


`r emo::ji("check")` Extract tabular data from PDFs using `tabulapdf`.

`r emo::ji("check")` Use vision APIs to interpret images and infographics.

`r emo::ji("check")` Extract structured information from documents with LLM-based parsing.

---

## Kahoot/Quiz Review: Images & Documents

.pull-left[

**Q1.** Which R package is commonly used to extract tables from PDF files?
A. `rvest`
B. `tabulapdf`
C. `jsonlite`
D. `httr2`

**Q2.** What does a "vision API" typically accept as input?
A. A CSV file
B. An image file or URL
C. A SQL query
D. An HTML page

]

.pull-right[

**Q3.** Why is LLM-based extraction useful for unstructured documents?
A. It is always faster than regex
B. It can understand context and infer structure
C. It requires no API key
D. It only works on JSON files

**Q4.** When extracting data from an infographic, which approach is most reliable?
A. Manually copying numbers
B. Using OCR + LLM to interpret layout and values
C. Downloading the raw CSV embedded in the image
D. Using `read_csv()` directly on the image URL

]

`r countdown(minutes = 3, warn_when = 60, color_background = miamired, color_text = "white", font_size = "1.5em", top = 0, right = 0)`


---

# Learning Objectives for Today's Class

- **LO1:** Define the principles of tidy data

- **LO2:** Reshape data using `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()`

- **LO3:** Perform rectangling and unnesting operations

- **LO4:** Validate data types and value constraints

- **LO5:** Detect and handle duplicates


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# LO1: The Principles of Tidy Data `r emo::ji('broom')`

---

## What Is Tidy Data?

.pull-left[

Tidy data is a **standard way of organizing tabular data** so that:

1. Each **variable** forms a column.
2. Each **observation** forms a row.
3. Each **value** has its own cell.

These three rules make data **consistently structured**, which means the same tools work across different datasets.

> "Tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way." -- Hadley Wickham

]

.pull-right[

```{r tidy-illustration, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('https://github.com/allisonhorst/stats-illustrations/raw/main/rstats-artwork/tidydata_1.jpg')
```

]

.footnote[
<html><hr></html>
**Source:** Illustration from the Openscapes blog, [Tidy Data for reproducibility, efficiency, and collaboration](https://www.openscapes.org/blog/2020/10/12/tidy-data/) by Julia Lowndes and Allison Horst.
]

---

## The Three Rules Visualized

```{r tidy-rules, echo=FALSE, out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics('https://github.com/allisonhorst/stats-illustrations/raw/main/rstats-artwork/tidydata_2.jpg')
```

.footnote[
<html><hr></html>
**Source:** Illustration from the Openscapes blog, [Tidy Data for reproducibility, efficiency, and collaboration](https://www.openscapes.org/blog/2020/10/12/tidy-data/) by Julia Lowndes and Allison Horst.
]

---

## Tidy vs. Messy: A Job Market Example

.pull-left[

**Messy (wide) format:** Salary columns spread across years.

```{r messy-example, echo=FALSE}
messy_jobs <- tibble::tribble(
  ~job_title,        ~salary_2023, ~salary_2024, ~salary_2025,
  "Data Analyst",     65000,        68000,        72000,
  "Data Scientist",   95000,        100000,       108000,
  "BI Developer",     78000,        82000,        87000
)
knitr::kable(messy_jobs, format = "html")
```

]

.pull-right[

**Tidy (long) format:** One row per observation.

```{r tidy-example, echo=FALSE}
tidy_jobs <- messy_jobs |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = dplyr::starts_with("salary_"),
    names_to = "year",
    names_prefix = "salary_",
    values_to = "salary"
  )
knitr::kable(tidy_jobs, format = "html")
```

]

--

.font80[
The tidy format is better because: each variable (job title, year, salary) has its own column, and each row is one observation (a job in a specific year).
]


---

## Activity: Tidy or Not?

`r countdown(minutes = 4, seconds = 0, top = 0, font_size = "2em")`

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Activity]

.small[
Examine the five `tidyr` built-in tables below (`table1` through `table4b`). Each displays TB cases from Afghanistan, Brazil, and China (1999--2000).

- **Identify which table(s) are tidy.**
- **For non-tidy tables, state which rule(s) are violated.**
- Discuss with a neighbor.
]
]

.panel[.panel-name[`table1`]

```{r activity-table1, echo=FALSE}
print(tidyr::table1, width = 80)
```

.can-edit.key-activity01[
Tidy or not? ..............................
Rules broken (if any): ..............................
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[`table2`]

```{r activity-table2, echo=FALSE}
print(tidyr::table2, width = 80)
```

.can-edit.key-activity02[
Tidy or not? ..............................
Rules broken (if any): ..............................
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[`table3`]

```{r activity-table3, echo=FALSE}
print(tidyr::table3, width = 80)
```

.can-edit.key-activity03[
Tidy or not? ..............................
Rules broken (if any): ..............................
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[`table4a` & `table4b`]

```{r activity-table4, echo=FALSE}
cat("table4a (cases):")
print(tidyr::table4a, width = 80)
cat("\ntable4b (population):")
print(tidyr::table4b, width = 80)
```

.can-edit.key-activity04[
Tidy or not? ..............................
Rules broken (if any): ..............................
]

]

]


---

## LO1 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now define tidy data by its three rules:

- Each variable forms a column

- Each observation forms a row

- Each value has its own cell


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# LO2: Reshaping Data with `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()`

---

## Wide vs. Long Data

```{r wide-vs-long-graphic, echo=FALSE, out.width='50%'}
knitr::include_graphics('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/main/images/static/png/original-dfs-tidy.png')
```

.footnote[
<html><hr></html>
**Source:** Garrick Aden-Buie's [tidyexplain GitHub Repository](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain).
]


---

## The `pivot_longer()` Function

- **Use case:** When column names contain values (e.g., year columns `salary_2023`, `salary_2024`).

- **Three key arguments:**
  * `cols` -- which columns to pivot
  * `names_to` -- name for the new "key" column
  * `values_to` -- name for the new "value" column

```{r pivot-longer-animation, echo=FALSE, out.width='40%'}
knitr::include_graphics('https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/raw/main/images/tidyr-pivoting.gif')
```

.footnote[
<html><hr></html>
**Source:** Garrick Aden-Buie's [tidyexplain GitHub Repository](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain).
]


---

## Demo: `pivot_longer()` with Job Market Data

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[The Data]

.font80[
Suppose we scraped BLS wage data where each year is a separate column -- a common format in government data.
]

```{r pivot-longer-data}
wages_wide <- tibble::tribble(
  ~occupation,         ~avg_wage_2022, ~avg_wage_2023, ~avg_wage_2024,
  "Data Analyst",       62000,          65000,          68000,
  "Software Developer", 92000,          97000,          102000,
  "BI Analyst",         70000,          73000,          77000,
  "Database Admin",     75000,          78000,          82000
)
wages_wide
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[The Code]

```{r pivot-longer-code}
wages_long <- wages_wide |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = dplyr::starts_with("avg_wage_"),   #<< columns to pivot
    names_to = "year",                 #<< new column for the year
    names_prefix = "avg_wage_",        #<< strip prefix from names
    values_to = "avg_wage"             #<< new column for values
  )
wages_long
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Why It Matters]

.font80[
- The long format enables easy grouping, filtering, and plotting by year.

- Try this: `ggplot(wages_long, aes(x = year, y = avg_wage, color = occupation)) + geom_line(aes(group = occupation))`

- Government data, BLS reports, and census tables almost always arrive in wide format -- you will need `pivot_longer()` regularly.
]

]

]


---

## The `pivot_wider()` Function

- **Use case:** When an observation is scattered across multiple rows (e.g., `table2` where "cases" and "population" are stacked).

- **Two key arguments:**
  * `names_from` -- column whose values become new column names
  * `values_from` -- column whose values fill the new columns

```{r pivot-wider-illustration, echo=FALSE, out.width='25%'}
knitr::include_graphics('https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain/raw/main/images/static/png/tidyr-pivot_wider.png')
```

.footnote[
<html><hr></html>
**Source:** Garrick Aden-Buie's [tidyexplain GitHub Repository](https://github.com/gadenbuie/tidyexplain).
]


---

## Demo: `pivot_wider()` with Stacked Metrics

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[The Data]

```{r pivot-wider-data}
job_metrics_long <- tibble::tribble(
  ~company,    ~metric,         ~value,
  "Google",    "avg_salary",    155000,
  "Google",    "num_openings",  1200,
  "Meta",      "avg_salary",    162000,
  "Meta",      "num_openings",  850,
  "Amazon",    "avg_salary",    148000,
  "Amazon",    "num_openings",  2100
)
job_metrics_long
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[The Code]

```{r pivot-wider-code}
job_metrics_wide <- job_metrics_long |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = metric,      #<< column values become new column names
    values_from = value        #<< values fill the new columns
  )
job_metrics_wide
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[When to Use]

.font80[
- Use `pivot_wider()` when you need to **compare metrics side by side** for each entity.

- Common in dashboards where each row should represent one company/person/product.

- **Rule of thumb:** If the same entity appears in multiple rows with different "type" labels, you likely need `pivot_wider()`.
]

]

]


---

## Activity: Pivot BLS Wage Data

`r countdown(minutes = 5, seconds = 0, top = 0, font_size = "2em")`

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Instructions]

.font80[
1. The code below creates a wide-format dataset of mean hourly wages by state and occupation.
2. **Pivot it to long format** so each row is one state-occupation-wage observation.
3. Then **filter** to only occupations containing "Data" and **arrange** by wage descending.
]

```{r pivot-activity-starter, eval=FALSE}
state_wages <- tibble::tribble(
  ~state,  ~data_analyst, ~data_scientist, ~bi_developer,
  "OH",     32.50,         48.75,           38.20,
  "CA",     42.10,         62.30,           49.50,
  "NY",     40.80,         58.90,           47.10,
  "TX",     34.20,         50.10,           40.30
)

# Your code: pivot_longer, then filter & arrange
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Your Solution]

.can-edit.key-activity05[
```
# Write your solution here
```
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[Reference Solution]

```{r pivot-activity-solution, eval=FALSE}
state_wages |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = -state,
               names_to = "occupation",
               values_to = "hourly_wage") |>
  dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(occupation, "data")) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(hourly_wage))
```

]

]


---

## LO2 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now reshape data using:

- `pivot_longer()` to convert wide data to long (column names become values)

- `pivot_wider()` to convert long data to wide (row values become column names)

- `names_prefix` to strip common prefixes during pivoting


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# LO3: Rectangling and Unnesting

---

## What Is Rectangling?

- **Rectangling** is the process of converting **deeply nested data** (e.g., JSON from APIs or LLM outputs) into flat, rectangular data frames.

- The `tidyr` package provides three key functions:

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `unnest_wider()` | Turns each element of a list-column into its own column |
| `unnest_longer()` | Turns each element of a list-column into its own row |
| `hoist()` | Selectively pulls components from a list-column |

- **Why this matters:** API responses and LLM-extracted data often arrive as nested lists. Rectangling turns them into analysis-ready tables.


---

## Demo: Unnesting Nested API Data

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[The Nested Data]

.font80[
Imagine we received this nested JSON response from a jobs API and parsed it with `jsonlite::fromJSON()`:
]

```{r rectangling-data}
jobs_nested <- tibble::tibble(
  company = c("Acme Corp", "Beta Inc"),
  postings = list(
    tibble::tibble(title = c("Data Analyst", "BI Developer"),
           salary_min = c(60000, 70000),
           salary_max = c(80000, 95000)),
    tibble::tibble(title = c("Data Scientist"),
           salary_min = c(95000),
           salary_max = c(130000))
  )
)
jobs_nested
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Unnesting]

```{r rectangling-unnest}
jobs_flat <- jobs_nested |>
  tidyr::unnest(postings)  #<< unnest the list-column

jobs_flat
```

.font80[
Each posting now gets its own row, with the parent `company` repeated as needed.
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[`unnest_wider` Example]

```{r unnest-wider-example}
# When each list element is a named list (like a single record)
company_info <- tibble::tibble(
  company = c("Acme Corp", "Beta Inc"),
  details = list(
    list(industry = "Tech", founded = 2005, employees = 500),
    list(industry = "Finance", founded = 2010, employees = 200)
  )
)

company_info |> tidyr::unnest_wider(details) #<< each list key becomes a column
```

]

]


---

## Demo: `unnest_longer()` for Repeated Elements

```{r unnest-longer-demo}
# When each list element contains a vector of values
skills_data <- tibble::tibble(
  job_title = c("Data Analyst", "Data Scientist"),
  required_skills = list(
    c("SQL", "Excel", "R", "Tableau"),
    c("Python", "R", "TensorFlow", "SQL", "Docker")
  )
)

skills_data |>
  tidyr::unnest_longer(required_skills) #<< each skill becomes its own row
```

.font80[
This is extremely useful for analyzing skill frequencies across job postings -- a common BI task.
]


---

## LO3 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now perform rectangling operations using:

- `unnest()` to flatten list-columns of data frames

- `unnest_wider()` to expand named lists into columns

- `unnest_longer()` to expand vectors/lists into rows


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# LO4: Validating Data Types and Value Constraints

---

## Why Validate Data?

- **Tidy data is NOT the same as clean data.** Data can be perfectly tidy (correct structure) but still contain:
  * Wrong data types (salary stored as character)
  * Impossible values (negative salaries, salary_min > salary_max)
  * Missing values in critical fields
  * Constraint violations (percentages > 100)

- Validation is the process of **checking data against rules** and reporting violations.

- In production BI systems, validation pipelines run **automatically** before data enters dashboards or models.


---

## The `pointblank` Package `r fontawesome::fa('box', fill='gold')`

- The [pointblank](https://rstudio.github.io/pointblank/) package provides a **data validation framework** for R.

- Workflow:
  1. **Create an agent** -- attach it to your data
  2. **Define validation steps** -- specify rules for columns
  3. **Interrogate** -- run all checks and get a report

```{r pointblank-workflow, eval=FALSE}
agent <- pointblank::create_agent(my_data) |>          # Step 1
  pointblank::col_is_numeric(columns = vars(salary)) |> # Step 2
  pointblank::col_vals_gt(columns = vars(salary), 0) |>
  pointblank::col_vals_lte(columns = vars(pct), 100) |>
  pointblank::interrogate()                             # Step 3
```

.footnote[
<html><hr></html>
**Source:** [pointblank documentation](https://rstudio.github.io/pointblank/).
]


---

## Demo: Validating Job Market Data

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[The Data]

```{r validation-data}
job_data <- tibble::tribble(
  ~job_id, ~title,            ~salary_min, ~salary_max, ~experience_yrs, ~remote_pct,
  1,       "Data Analyst",     55000,       75000,       2,               80,
  2,       "BI Developer",     70000,       65000,       3,               50,
  3,       "Data Scientist",   -5000,       120000,      5,               110,
  4,       "ML Engineer",      95000,       140000,      NA,              100,
  5,       "Data Analyst",     55000,       75000,       2,               80,
  6,       "Analytics Mgr",    85000,       115000,      7,               40
)
job_data
```

.font80[
Can you spot the issues? Row 2 has min > max, row 3 has negative salary and remote_pct > 100, row 4 has missing experience, and rows 1 & 5 are duplicates.
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[Validation Code]

```{r validation-code, eval=FALSE}
act <- pointblank::action_levels(warn_at = 0.01, stop_at = NULL)

agent <- pointblank::create_agent(job_data, actions = act) |>
  pointblank::col_is_numeric(columns = vars(salary_min, salary_max)) |>         #<< type check
  pointblank::col_vals_gt(columns = vars(salary_min, salary_max), value = 0) |> #<< positive salaries
  pointblank::col_vals_lte(columns = vars(salary_min),                          #<< min <= max
               columns(salary_max)) |>
  pointblank::col_vals_between(columns = vars(remote_pct), 0, 100) |>           #<< pct in range
  pointblank::col_vals_not_null(columns = vars(experience_yrs)) |>              #<< no NAs
  pointblank::interrogate()

agent
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Using Expressions]

```{r validation-expr, eval=FALSE}
# For cross-column checks, use col_vals_expr()
agent2 <- pointblank::create_agent(job_data, actions = act) |>
  pointblank::col_vals_expr(expr = expr(salary_min <= salary_max)) |>  #<< cross-column
  pointblank::col_vals_expr(expr = expr(salary_min > 0)) |>
  pointblank::col_vals_expr(expr = expr(remote_pct >= 0 & remote_pct <= 100)) |>
  pointblank::interrogate()

agent2
```

.font80[
`col_vals_expr()` is the most flexible validator -- it accepts any logical expression involving one or more columns.
]

]

]


---

## LO4 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now validate data using `pointblank` by:

- Checking column types with `col_is_numeric()`, `col_is_character()`, etc.

- Checking value constraints with `col_vals_gt()`, `col_vals_between()`, etc.

- Checking cross-column rules with `col_vals_expr()`


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# LO5: Detecting and Handling Duplicates

---

## Why Duplicates Matter

- Duplicate rows can **inflate counts**, **bias averages**, and **corrupt joins**.

- Common sources of duplicates in job market data:
  * The same posting scraped from multiple pages
  * API pagination returning overlapping results
  * Data entry errors

- R provides several tools for detection and removal.


---

## Detecting Duplicates with `dplyr`

```{r duplicates-demo}
# Our job_data from earlier -- rows 1 and 5 are identical
job_data |>
  dplyr::group_by(title, salary_min, salary_max, experience_yrs, remote_pct) |>
  dplyr::filter(dplyr::n() > 1) |>     #<< keep only groups with more than 1 row
  dplyr::ungroup()
```

--

**Removing duplicates:**

```{r duplicates-remove}
job_data_deduped <- job_data |>
  dplyr::distinct(title, salary_min, salary_max, experience_yrs, remote_pct,
                  .keep_all = TRUE) #<< keep all columns, remove dup rows

nrow(job_data)
nrow(job_data_deduped)
```


---

## LO5 Check-In

`r emo::ji("check")` You can now detect and handle duplicates using:

- `dplyr::distinct()` to remove exact duplicates

- `dplyr::group_by() |> filter(n() > 1)` to inspect duplicates before removal

- `janitor::get_dupes()` for quick duplicate detection


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Comprehensive Activity: Tidy, Validate, Deduplicate

---

## Activity: End-to-End Data Quality Pipeline

`r countdown(minutes = 8, seconds = 0, top = 0, font_size = "2em")`

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Instructions]

.font80[
You will take messy job posting data and apply the full pipeline:

1. **Pivot** the data from wide to long format
2. **Validate** that salaries are positive and min <= max
3. **Remove duplicates**
4. **Report** a data quality score (% of rows passing all checks)
]

```{r activity-end-to-end-data, eval=FALSE}
messy_postings <- tibble::tribble(
  ~company,     ~role,              ~salary_q1, ~salary_q2, ~salary_q3, ~salary_q4,
  "Acme",       "Data Analyst",      62000,      63000,      65000,      68000,
  "Acme",       "Data Analyst",      62000,      63000,      65000,      68000,
  "Beta",       "BI Developer",      75000,      -1000,      78000,      80000,
  "Gamma",      "Data Scientist",    95000,      98000,      102000,     105000
)
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Your Solution]

.can-edit.key-activity06[
```
# Step 1: Pivot to long

# Step 2: Remove duplicates

# Step 3: Validate

# Step 4: Compute quality score
```
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[Reference Solution]

```{r activity-end-to-end-solution, eval=FALSE}
clean_postings <- messy_postings |>
  # Step 1: Pivot
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = dplyr::starts_with("salary_"),
               names_to = "quarter", values_to = "salary") |>
  # Step 2: Deduplicate
  dplyr::distinct() |>
  # Step 3: Validate
  dplyr::mutate(is_valid = salary > 0)

# Step 4: Quality score
quality_score <- mean(clean_postings$is_valid) * 100
cat("Data quality score:", quality_score, "%")
```

]

]


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Recap

---

## Summary of Main Points

By now, you should be able to do the following:

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO1:** Define the three principles of tidy data (variables in columns, observations in rows, values in cells)

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO2:** Reshape data using `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()`

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO3:** Perform rectangling with `unnest()`, `unnest_wider()`, and `unnest_longer()`

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO4:** Validate data types and value constraints using `pointblank`

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO5:** Detect and handle duplicates with `distinct()` and `get_dupes()`

---

## Job Market Connection

.pull-left[

> "Clean, validated data is what separates analysts from data entry."

- **80% of data work** is getting data into the right shape.

- Employers expect you to deliver **trustworthy data** -- that means tidy structure, validated values, and no surprise duplicates.

- The pipeline you learned today (tidy, validate, deduplicate) is the foundation of every **production BI system**.

]

.pull-right[

```{r advantages-tidy, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('https://github.com/allisonhorst/stats-illustrations/raw/main/rstats-artwork/tidydata_5.jpg')
```

.footnote[
**Source:** Illustration by Julia Lowndes and Allison Horst.
]

]


---

## Readings and Resources

.pull-left[
.center[[<img src="https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/b88ef926a004b0fce72b2526b0b5c4413666a4cb/24a30/cover.png" height="300px">](https://r4ds.had.co.nz)]

* [Tidy Data](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/tidy-data.html) -- R for Data Science
* [Pivoting vignette](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/pivot.html)
]

.pull-right[
* [Rectangling vignette](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/rectangle.html)
* [{tidyr} cheatsheet](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/blob/main/tidyr.pdf)
* [pointblank documentation](https://rstudio.github.io/pointblank/)
]


---

## Things to Do to Prepare for Next Class

- **Read:** [Introduction to Data Cleaning with R](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/contrib/de_Jonge+van_der_Loo-Introduction_to_data_cleaning_with_R.pdf), Sections 3--4

- **Try:** The [Pivot Playground Shiny App](../apps/pivot_playground/) to practice `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()` interactively.

- **Next class:** Cleaning & Imputation -- handling missing data, imputation techniques, and text standardization.
